posts:
Field note: The more research I read, the more I think many people aren't running out of time.

They're running out of energy to make decisions.

By the end of a busy day, even small choices can start to feel surprisingly heavy.

threads_posts.text:
Field note: The more research I read, the more I think many people aren't running out of time.

They're running out of energy to make decisions.

By the end of a busy day, even small choices can start to feel surprisingly heavy.

posts:
One pattern showing up repeatedly in decision fatigue research: People rarely say they have too much work.

They often say they have too many things competing for attention.

The challenge isn't always doing the work. Sometimes it's deciding which piece of work deserves attention first.

threads_posts.text:
One pattern showing up repeatedly in decision fatigue research: People rarely say they have too much work.

They often say they have too many things competing for attention.

The challenge isn't always doing the work. Sometimes it's deciding which piece of work deserves attention first.

posts:
A small example: Imagine ending the day with ten unfinished tasks.

Completing one of them might only take fifteen minutes. Deciding which one to start can sometimes take longer.

That distinction feels important.

threads_posts.text:

posts: Field note: A surprising amount of organization is really pre-deciding.

threads_posts.text: Field note: A surprising amount of organization is really pre-deciding.

posts:
Something I've been noticing: Many routines exist because they remove a choice.

Morning routines. Weekly meal plans. Recurring calendar blocks.

They may look like productivity systems from the outside. Often they're really decision-reduction systems.

threads_posts.text:

Morning routines. Weekly meal plans. Recurring calendar blocks.

They may look like productivity systems from the outside. Often they're really decision-reduction systems.

posts:
The people I see struggling with overwhelm are rarely lazy. They're usually carrying a large number of open loops, competing priorities, and unfinished decisions.

That's a very different problem.

threads_posts.text:
The people I see struggling with overwhelm are rarely lazy. They're usually carrying a large number of open loops, competing priorities, and unfinished decisions.

That's a very different problem.

posts: Field note: People usually ask for better options. What they often need is fewer options.

threads_posts.text: Field note: People usually ask for better options. What they often need is fewer options.

posts:
One of the strongest findings from this week's research: People regularly create artificial limits for themselves.

Not because they enjoy restrictions, but because choosing between twenty possibilities is mentally expensive.

Choosing between three is often much easier.

threads_posts.text:

Choosing between three is often much easier.

posts:
I've started paying attention to how often people simplify their own lives through defaults.

The default lunch.
The default workout.
The default grocery list.

Defaults don't remove freedom. They remove unnecessary negotiation.

threads_posts.text:

posts: Field note: Mental load often looks invisible because so much of it happens inside someone's head.

threads_posts.text: Field note: Mental load often looks invisible because so much of it happens inside someone's head.

posts:
A lot of conversations about productivity focus on execution.

Research keeps pointing me toward something else.

Remembering. Prioritizing. Choosing. Following up.

Those activities consume a surprising amount of energy before any work actually begins.

threads_posts.text:
A lot of conversations about productivity focus on execution.

Research keeps pointing me toward something else.

Remembering. Prioritizing. Choosing. Following up.

Those activities consume a surprising amount of energy before any work actually begins.

posts:
Many people carry a quiet question throughout the day: 'What should I be working on right now?'

The work itself may be manageable. Answering that question over and over can become exhausting.

threads_posts.text:
Many people carry a quiet question throughout the day: 'What should I be working on right now?'

The work itself may be manageable. Answering that question over and over can become exhausting.

posts: Field note: Every recurring decision eventually becomes a candidate for a default.

threads_posts.text: Field note: Every recurring decision eventually becomes a candidate for a default.

posts:
Meal planning communities turned out to be unexpectedly interesting research.

Many meal plans aren't really about food.

They're about avoiding a daily decision.

The same pattern seems to appear in many other parts of life.

threads_posts.text:

The same pattern seems to appear in many other parts of life.

posts: A useful question: If a choice repeats every day, every week, or every month, does it really need to be made from scratch each time? Sometimes calm comes from deciding once instead of repeatedly.

threads_posts.text: A useful question: If a choice repeats every day, every week, or every month, does it really need to be made from scratch each time? Sometimes calm comes from deciding once instead of repeatedly.

posts: Field note: Overwhelm isn't always caused by having too much to do. Sometimes it's caused by having too many possible directions to go.

threads_posts.text: Field note: Overwhelm isn't always caused by having too much to do. Sometimes it's caused by having too many possible directions to go.

posts:
Something that keeps appearing across different communities: People trust routines.

Not because routines are exciting, but because routines remove uncertainty.

There's comfort in not having to renegotiate the same decision every day.

threads_posts.text:
Something that keeps appearing across different communities: People trust routines.

Not because routines are exciting, but because routines remove uncertainty.

There's comfort in not having to renegotiate the same decision every day.

posts:
One thing I'm learning from decision fatigue research: Clarity often comes from subtraction.

Fewer choices. Fewer open loops. Fewer competing priorities.

Less can be surprisingly helpful.

threads_posts.text:
One thing I'm learning from decision fatigue research: Clarity often comes from subtraction.

Fewer choices. Fewer open loops. Fewer competing priorities.

Less can be surprisingly helpful.

posts: Field note: The problem isn't always the task. Sometimes it's deciding that the task is the right thing to do next.

threads_posts.text: Field note: The problem isn't always the task. Sometimes it's deciding that the task is the right thing to do next.

posts:
Checklist worth considering: If you're feeling overwhelmed, ask:

• Do I have too much work?
• Or too many choices?
• Am I executing?
• Or constantly prioritizing?

Those aren't always the same problem.

This week's biggest takeaway: Many people seem to be building little systems whose primary purpose is helping them decide less. That feels like an important clue. Maybe calmer workflows begin with fewer decisions, not more features.
